text:
studies must have presented the means and standard devia-
tions of either change scores or end point scores for the drug
and placebo groups.
The concept of effect size was developed to facilitate
comparisons of efficacy between studies. Without using this
concept, comparing 2 studies can be difficult. Consider this
example. If one study measures efficacy using the Conners
ADHD Rating Scale and another uses the SNAP ADHD rating
scale and both report improvement scores, we cannot compare
the results because a 1-point improvement on the Conners
Scale is not equivalent to a 1-point improvement on the SNAP
scale. Even if 2 studies use the same scale, we cannot simply
compare mean change scores between drug and placebo
because these studies may differ in their precision of
measurement. We should have more faith in precise measures
than imprecise ones. These problems of differing scales of
measurement and differing precision of measurement make it
difficult to compare treatment studies. Fortunately, these
problems are overcome by the computation of effect size,
which gives us the difference in improvement between
drug and placebo adjusted for the scale and accuracy of
the measurements used in each study.
Effect sizes for each dependent measure in each study
were expressed as the standardized mean difference (SMD)
between drug and placebo outcome scores. The SMD is
computed by taking the mean outcome score of the active drug
group minus the mean outcome score of the placebo group and
dividing the result by the pooled standard deviation of the
groups. Studies reporting change scores provided end point
minus baseline scores for drug and placebo groups. In this
case, the SMD is computed as the difference between change
scores. For studies reporting end point scores, the SMD is
computed as the difference between end point scores. Studies
were weighted by the precision of their SMD estimate (which
is proportional to the study sample size). The meta-analysis
used the random effects model of DerSimonian and Laird.10
To determine if the results of the meta-analysis were unduly
influenced by one study, we conducted a sensitivity analysis
by recomputing the pooled SMD after deleting each study
observation of drug-placebo differences one at a time. Meta-
analytic regression determined if the effect sizes varied with
study design features.
Meta-analyses can be misleading if the available
literature is biased toward excluding negative studies. This
might occur, for example, if an investigator decided to not
write up results of a negative study. We assessed publication
bias using the method of Egger et al,11 which regresses the
standardized effect size (the standardized effect size divided
by its standard error) against the precision of the SMD (the
inverse of its standard error). In the absence of bias, the
regression of the standardized effect size on the precision
should plot a line with positive slope that runs through the
origin (ie, small samples with low precision should have
large standard errors and small standardized effect sizes; large
samples have higher precision, smaller standard errors, and
should have large standardized effect sizes). Egger et al’s
publication bias statistic is the intercept of the regression,
which will be significantly greater than zero in the presence
of bias.
Several studies provided data on more than one measure
(eg, hyperactive-impulsive symptoms and inattentive symp-
toms). Because measures reported from the same study are not
statistically independent from one another, standard statistical
procedures will produce inaccurate P values. To address this
intrafamily clustering, variance estimates were adjusted using
the formula of Huber12 as implemented in Stata.13 This
formula is a ‘‘theoretical bootstrap’’ that produces robust
statistical tests. The method works by adjusting the estimate of
variance to account for nonindependent observations. The
resulting P values are valid even when observations are not
statistically independent of one another.
RESULTS
Six articles met criteria for inclusion in the meta-
analysis.14–19 These studies included a total of 140 MPH-
treated ADHD adults and 113 placebo-treated ADHD adults.
Because some articles reported data on more than one
outcome score, the 6 articles provided 10 drug-placebo
comparisons that could be used for computing effect sizes.
Table 1 describes the main features of each study. Table 2
gives the effect size for each study expressed as the SMD
between the MPH and placebo conditions. The mean effect
size of 0.9 was statistically significant (z = 4.3, P < 0.001). Our
sensitivity analysis found that no one observation accounted
for the statistically significant mean effect size, ie, the mean
effect size remained significant when computed after deleting
each observation one at a time (all P’s > 0.05). The Egger
publication bias statistic was small (0.5) and not statistically
significant (t8 = 0.2, P = 0.9). The publication bias graph in
Figure 1 shows the expected pattern; the larger, more precise
studies yielded larger standardized effect sizes and thus more
evidence for drug-placebo differences. The effect sizes in
Table 2 range from a low of 0.24 to a high of 2.3. This
variability among studies was statistically significant (χ² = 47,
P < 0.001), which indicates the presence of systematic
differences among studies. We searched for possible expla-
nations of between study differences using meta-analysis
regression to predict effect sizes from study features. These
results are given in Table 3, which shows significant effects
for type of rater and dose but not for study design (crossover
vs. parallel). Larger MPH effect sizes were associated with
physician ratings of outcome and use of higher doses. We de-
fined high-dose MPH studies as those having a mean dose of
0.9 mg/kg/day or greater. The mean daily doses were 44 mg
(0.63 mg/kg) for the low-dose group and 70 mg (1.05 mg/kg)

for the high-dose group. Kuperman et al19 did not provide daily dose data and was not included in these analyses. The effect size for crossover studies was greater than the effect size for parallel studies but the difference was not significant. The following variables were not associated with the MPH effect size: date of study, proportion of male subjects in the sample, and length of the study protocol (all P's > 0.05).

DISCUSSION

Our meta-analysis of double-blind placebo-controlled studies of the efficacy of MPH for adult ADHD supports the assertion that MPH is efficacious for the treatment of adult ADHD, particularly when used in weight-corrected doses equipotent to those used in pediatric studies. The pooled effect size across studies (0.9) is comparable to those computed in the meta-analysis of Schachter et al4 of 62 studies of MPH in children and adolescents, which reported significant effect sizes for teacher (0.8) and parent (0.5) reports of changes in ADHD symptoms. The analysis of Smith et al5 of 8 adolescent studies found a mean effect size of 0.9. The mean effect size from our meta-analysis (0.9) shows the effect of MPH for adults with ADHD is in the same range as that reported for children and adolescents with ADHD and that these positive results cannot be attributed to publication biases.

Because effect sizes compare results across different studies using different measures, it cannot be interpreted as a difference in rating scale points or percent improvement. How then are we to interpret SMDs? One approach is to use the widely accepted guidelines of Cohen (1988).20 For SMDs computed by research in the behavioral sciences, he defined 0.2 as small, 0.5 as medium, and 0.8 as large. He gives the mean height difference between 15-year-old and 16-year-old girls, which is 1/2 in, as an example of a small effect size. The height difference between 14-year-old and 18-year-old girls

Journal of Clinical Psychopharmacology • Volume 24, Number 1, February 2004          Meta-Analysis of Medications for ADHD

(about 1 in) is his example of a medium effect size and the height difference between 13-year-old and 18-year-old girls (about 1 1/2 in) is a large effect size. Another example can be made from differences in intelligence as measured by the Wechsler IQ scales. If a medication increased IQ by 3 points, we would say it had a small effect. We would call an increase of 7 1/2 IQ points a medium effect and an increase of 12 IQ points a large effect.

Our meta-analysis excluded studies not meeting our inclusion criteria. We excluded 2 open trials. One reported a 70% response rate using doses of 40 mg/day.21 The other was a study of cocaine abusers with ADHD. It showed improvement of ADHD symptoms, reduction of cocaine cravings, and no abuse of MPH.22 We excluded the double-blind parallel study of Wood et al23 because they did not use structured diagnostic criteria. Instead, they used the diagnosis of minimal brain dysfunction to select patients for study. Using a low mean dose of 27 mg/day, they found a 53% improvement rate in MPH-treated adults with minimal brain dysfunction. We excluded the double-blind crossover study of Iaboni et al24 because it did not provide the data needed to compute an effect size. These authors reported moderate improvement with MPH using relatively low doses of 30 to 45 mg/day. Thus, it is reassuring that, as a group, the excluded studies are consistent with our meta-analysis in supporting the efficacy of high-dose MPH in adults with ADHD.

One useful methodological finding from our meta-analysis regression was that physician ratings led to higher effect sizes compared with self-ratings. Although self-ratings may be sufficiently reliable and sensitive in some contexts, they do not appear to be as useful as physician ratings for detecting response to MPH. We also found that higher doses led to nearly twice the effect size than lower doses (1.3 vs. 0.7). We defined high-dose MPH studies as those having a mean dose of 0.9 mg/kg/day or greater. The mean daily doses were 44 mg (0.63 mg/kg) for the low-dose group and 70 mg (1.05 mg/kg) for the high-dose group.

Although we were not surprised to find that high doses were more efficacious than low doses, these results should assure those clinicians who remain skeptical about the diagnosis of ADHD in adults. Because, compared with children, symptoms in adults are less disruptive, case identification requires careful interviewing about symptoms of inattention, restlessness, impulsivity, and disorganization.25 Moreover, diagnosing ADHD in adulthood requires clinicians to make an accurate retrospective diagnosis of childhood onset ADHD, which may be difficult in older patients. Some studies suggest retrospective diagnoses are valid26–28 but others question their validity.29,30 And, prior to the completion of high-dose MPH studies of adults, the lower efficacy reported by the early MPH studies raised further concerns about the validity of the adult ADHD diagnosis.30 Because of these concerns, the disorder has not been routinely assessed in clinical settings, as seen in our survey of primary care clinics, which showed low identification and treatment of adults with ADHD.31

We also found significant differences among studies in the MPH effect size. Notably, studies using the patient's self-ratings of ADHD symptoms to assess efficacy resulted in low effect sizes. This suggests that such ratings may not be as informative for detecting change in ADHD symptoms as clinician-based rating. Thus, in clinical and research settings, clinician ratings should be used to assess symptom change during treatment. Our finding of a statistically significant effect of dose provides quantitative support for the hypothesis of Spencer et al17 that early studies of MPH use in adult ADHD had not found robust response rates because they used low doses. Our meta-analysis shows that when treatment is optimized to high doses, the effect size for MPH in adults is 1.3. Lower doses led to substantially lower efficacy (0.7). The importance of optimal dosing for assuring high efficacy has also been stressed in MPH studies of ADHD youth. Most notable in this regard are results from the large NIMH Multimodal Treatment of ADHD study.32 The Multimodal Treatment of ADHD study compared ADHD youth treated in the community with ADHD youth who had treatment optimized by the study investigators. Compared with the community-treated group, the group treated by the study investigators received more doses per day, higher daily doses, and showed more improvement in their ADHD symptoms.

This work must be interpreted in light of several limitations. Compared with studies of child and adolescent ADHD, there are few MPH treatment studies of adult ADHD. Thus, the sample size for our meta-analysis was small. Because meta-analysis relies on data collected by others, the validity and strength of our conclusions relies on the quality of the individual studies, which was beyond our control.

© 2004 Lippincott Williams & Wilkins                                                                                                                    27

Copyright © Lippincott Williams & Wilkins. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.
Although our inclusion criteria required each study to meet criteria for study quality (use of double-blind placebo-controlled methods, selection of subjects with structured diagnostic criteria), other features of these studies may account for some of the interstudy variability in results.

Another limitation is that our analysis did not address the use of other medications for adult ADHD. We could not do this because there are not enough data for any one of these to conduct a meta-analysis. Even so, clinicians should note that efficacy for adult ADHD has been shown for atomoxetine,33 mixed amphetamine salts,34 tricyclic antidepressants,35–37 and bupropion.38,39

Although these limitations stress the need for further pharmacotherapy studies of adult ADHD, they do not undermine the key conclusions of this study. MPH shows high efficacy for adult ADHD, especially when doses are optimized to high levels. Because the efficacy of MPH for adult ADHD is similar to that reported from meta-analyses of the child and adolescent literature, our work provides further assurance to clinicians that the diagnosis of ADHD can be validly applied in adulthood.

ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

This work was supported, in part, by a grant from McNeil Consumer and Specialty Pharmaceuticals to Stephen Faraone PhD.

ORIGINAL CONTRIBUTION

Meta-Analysis of the Efficacy of Methylphenidate for Treating Adult Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder

Stephen V. Faraone, PhD,*†‡ Thomas Spencer, MD,*† Megan Aleardi,* Christine Pagano,* and Joseph Biederman, MD*†

Abstract: This article reviews the efficacy of methylphenidate (MPH) for adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). A literature search identified double-blind placebo-controlled MPH treatment studies of ADHD adults. Meta-analysis estimated the pooled effect size for MPH treatment and tested for publication bias. Meta-analysis regression assessed the influence of study design features on medication effects. Six trials met criteria and were included in this meta-analysis. These studies included a total of 140 MPH-treated ADHD adults and 113 placebo-treated ADHD adults. The mean effect size of 0.9 was statistically significant and there was no evidence of publication bias. Larger MPH effect sizes were associated with physician ratings of outcome and use of higher doses. When treatment is optimized to high doses, the effect size for MPH in adults was 1.3. We found strong support for the assertion that MPH is efficacious for treating adult ADHD. Because the degree of efficacy of MPH in treating ADHD adults is similar to what has been reported from meta-analyses of the child and adolescent literature, our work provides further assurance to clinicians that the diagnosis of ADHD can be validly applied in adulthood.

(J Clin Psychopharmacol 2004;24:24–29)

For decades, methylphenidate (MPH) has been one of the first-line treatments for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), which, because of its associated impairments1 and high prevalence around the world,2 is a serious public health problem. MPH reduces the overactivity, impulsivity, and inattentiveness of ADHD patients and improves on-task behavior, academic performance, and social functioning.3

Many studies have assessed the efficacy of MPH for treating ADHD in childhood. Meta-analyses of these studies show the effect to be robust and statistically significant. By pooling data across studies, meta-analysis systematically evaluates the size and generalizability of drug effects. The meta-analysis of Schachter et al4 identified 62 randomized studies of MPH in patients aged 18 years and less (median age 8.7 years). The patients had been diagnosed with either attention-deficit disorder or ADHD. They found significant effect sizes for teacher (0.8) and parent (0.5) reports of changes in ADHD symptoms. Smith et al5 performed a systematic review of MPH treatment studies of adolescents and identified 8 placebo-controlled studies. These studies involved 214 adolescents in total and a mean duration of 6 weeks. All 8 studies reported statistically significant improvements in ADHD symptoms for MPH relative to placebo, with a mean effect size of 0.9. Consistent with the results of these efficacy studies, several practice guidelines recommend the use of MPH as first-line therapy for ADHD.6–9

In contrast to the meta-analyses of MPH response for childhood ADHD, no such analyses have been completed for adult ADHD. The present study seeks to fill this gap in the literature by applying meta-analysis to the literature on the MPH treatment of adult ADHD. Our goals were to estimate the effect size for MPH therapy in adults and determine if features of study design influenced the estimate of medication efficacy.

METHODS

We searched for relevant articles using the following search engines: Pubmed, Ovid, ERIC, Cinahl, MEDLINE, PreMEDLINE, the Cochrane database, e-psyche, and social sciences abstracts. We placed no limit on the year of publication. Citations from identified articles were searched for relevant studies. We only included studies using randomized, double-blind methodology with placebo controls. To assure the relevance of these studies to current diagnostic conceptions of ADHD and make the results comparable with meta-analyses of literature on children, we required studies to have selected patients using DSM-III, DSM-III-R, or DSM-IV diagnostic criteria. To be included,
